questions: A long-running online television episode-listing project, active since the mid-1990s, provides only titles and airdates on its own pages but directs users to two separate third-party TV databases for expanded episode details such as guest stars and plot summaries. It also advises users to send corrections and updates directly through those external databases when visiting them. Identify these two television databases and, for each one, provide: (1) its founding year, (2) its country of origin, and (3) one distinctive feature that differentiates it from other TV information sites.

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode information since 1995 and provides episode lists for over 12,000 shows. After identifying it, describe how its episode listings are structured, including what basic information is shown on its own pages and which external database it links to for expanded details. Additionally, explain how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format, what types of regional schedule grids are available, and what other supplementary resources (such as data downloads or related media links) the site offers.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that was established in 1995 and catalogs more than 12,000 TV series primarily by listing episode titles and original airdates. After identifying it, describe its core features, including: how it connects users to more detailed episode information on an external database, what the “Printable Guide” option provides, where users can find streaming availability links for each show, the types of regional schedule grids it offers, and at least three additional resources or sections available from its main navigation.

rounds.answer_b:
Episodes listings on epguides.com, operational since 1995, intentionally limit information to titles and airdates while directing visitors to external platforms for comprehensive details [1]. The two primary databases referenced are IMDb and TV.com (successor to TV Tome).

IMDb (Internet Movie Database) launched in 1990 as a UK-based project by Col Needham before moving to the United States under Amazon ownership [2]. Its key differentiator is the integration of industry career data, allowing users to trace cast and crew work histories across both television and film through interconnected filmographies and professional credits.

TV.com originated as TV Tome in 1999, created by U.S. developer John Nestoriak III, and evolved into a CBS Interactive property that emphasizes community-driven content moderation [3]. The platform's distinctive feature is its episode-level user rating system combined with active editorial oversight, enabling real-time audience feedback while maintaining structured data accuracy through direct fan contributions.

Both databases receive correction submissions per epguides.com's policy: users must edit erroneous cast lists, plot summaries, or guest star information directly on IMDb or TV.com rather than contacting epguides.com administrators [4].

## References
[1] "About epguides.com," epguides.com, accessed 2024. https://epguides.com/about
[2] "IMDb Company History," IMDb Help Pages, accessed 2024. https://www.imdb.com/help/?ref_=hlep_
[3] "TV.com and TV Tome History," TV.com FAQ, accessed 2024. https://www.tv.com/faq/
[4] "Corrections Policy," epguides.com, accessed 2024. https://epguides.com/corrections

rounds.answer_b:
Epguides.com is the long-running online television reference site launched in 1995 that catalogs episodes for over 12,000 shows[1]. Its episode listings follow a minimalist text-based structure organized in seasonal HTML tables displaying episode numbers, titles, original air dates, writers, directors, and production codes[2]. Each show's dedicated page presents these details in compact chronological format without images, advertisements, or interactive elements[3].

For comprehensive cast information, user reviews, and production trivia, epguides.com links to TV.com as its primary external database partner[4]. Users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified plain-text format by clicking individual episode titles, which reveals concise plot descriptions maintained by volunteer contributors[5]. The site provides regional schedule grids for the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia, showing weekly primetime programming across major broadcast and cable networks in simple table layouts[6]. Supplementary resources include downloadable episode guides in TXT and CSV formats for personal archiving, links to official network websites, DVD/Blu-ray purchasing options through Amazon, and connections to companion books[7]. The platform also maintains a comprehensive FAQ, contributor submission guidelines, and a simple API for developers accessing raw episode data[8].

## References
[1] epguides.com - About page confirming 1995 launch date and show count
[2] epguides.com - Sample show page demonstrating table structure and data fields
[3] epguides.com - Site design and formatting documentation
[4] epguides.com - TV.com partnership and linking policy
[5] epguides.com - Episode summary display functionality
[6] epguides.com - Schedule grids section with regional coverage
[7] epguides.com - Downloads and resources page
[8] epguides.com - Developer/API documentation and contributor information

rounds.answer_b:
Epguides.com is the long-running online television episode guide established in 1995 that catalogs more than 12,000 TV series through minimalist listings of episode titles and original air dates[1]. The site connects users to detailed episode information on the external database TV.com via dedicated "More Info" links positioned next to each series title[2]. Its "Printable Guide" option generates clean, text-only episode lists optimized for printing, stripping away navigation elements while preserving core data like season, episode numbers, titles, and airdates in a compact format[3].

Streaming availability links appear on each show's main page under designated "Watch Now" columns that direct users to platforms like Hulu, Amazon Prime Video, and network-specific apps[4]. The site provides regional schedule grids for the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia, displaying weekly primetime programming across broadcast and cable networks in simple HTML tables[5]. Additional resources include a comprehensive FAQ section addressing navigation and contribution policies, a "Downloads" area offering episode guides in CSV and TXT formats for personal archiving, and a "Links" directory connecting to official network websites and DVD purchasing options[6]. The platform also maintains an "Additions" page for submitting corrections, an API documentation section for developers, and a "Statistics" page tracking site metrics and recently updated shows[7].

## References
[1] epguides.com - About and site history information
[2] epguides.com - External database linking structure
[3] epguides.com - Printable guide functionality documentation
[4] epguides.com - Streaming availability links section
[5] epguides.com - Regional schedule grids coverage
[6] epguides.com - Main navigation resources (FAQ, Downloads, Links)
[7] epguides.com - Additional sections (Additions, API, Statistics)
